# AGENTS.md

> Generated by `mind-ontology emit` from `.agentctx/` — **do not hand-edit.**
> Edit the source files and re-run `mind-ontology emit`. Agents with MCP
> access get richer, task-scoped context from the live `get_context` tool.

## Constraints

### Keep the ontology portable <!-- (from .agentctx/constraints.md) -->
Mind Ontology source files should be readable by any AI agent or MCP client.
Avoid tool-specific assumptions unless the block is explicitly scoped to that
tool.

### No secrets in ontology files <!-- (from .agentctx/constraints.md) -->
Never store API keys, passwords, tokens, private keys, customer secrets, or
other credential material in `.agentctx/`. Use the ontology to describe safe
handling rules, not to store secret values.

### Confirm before destructive work <!-- (from .agentctx/constraints.md) -->
Before deleting data, rewriting history, changing production configuration, or
making irreversible changes, the agent must call `list_constraints()` and
follow the project-specific stop policy.

### Prefer small scoped context packs <!-- (from .agentctx/constraints.md) -->
Agents should receive the smallest context pack that can safely guide the task.
Do not dump the whole ontology into every session when `get_context(task)` can
select the relevant blocks.

## Identity

### Operator profile <!-- (from .agentctx/identity.md) -->
Describe who the AI is helping and how the AI should relate to them.

Example:

The operator is a builder who prefers autonomous execution, compact summaries,
and tools that turn repeated explanation into reusable context.

### Working style <!-- (from .agentctx/identity.md) -->
Describe the working rhythm that helps the operator.

Example:

Prefer concrete progress over abstract discussion. Ask for clarification only
when a product, cost, legal, or safety decision cannot be inferred from the
available context.

## Direction

### Current direction <!-- (from .agentctx/direction.md) -->
Describe what you are trying to build, improve, or decide right now.

Example:

The current focus is to turn a useful local AI workflow into a repeatable tool
that multiple AI agents can share. The near-term goal is a small, reliable MCP
server that gives agents the same direction and constraints for each task.

### Product posture <!-- (from .agentctx/direction.md) -->
Describe how this work should feel as a product or workflow.

Example:

The tool should feel practical and calm: one source of truth, short context
packs, and fewer repeated explanations across AI clients.

### Near-term priorities <!-- (from .agentctx/direction.md) -->
List the next few priorities in order.

Example:

1. Make the local context compiler dependable.
2. Add a minimal MCP server.
3. Create templates that a new user can edit.
4. Prove the same ontology can guide more than one AI client.

## Projects

### Active project <!-- (from .agentctx/projects.md) -->
Name: Example Project
Status: active

Describe what this project is, why it matters, and which repos, docs, or tools
belong to it.

### Secondary project <!-- (from .agentctx/projects.md) -->
Name: Example Secondary Project
Status: exploratory

Describe any adjacent project that agents should know about but not confuse
with the active project.

## Decisions

### Use one source for agent context <!-- (from .agentctx/decisions.md) -->
Status: accepted
Date: YYYY-MM-DD

Maintain one `.agentctx/` source instead of separately rewriting the same
direction, vocabulary, and constraints for every AI tool.

Reason:

Separate instruction files drift. A single source lets every agent start from
the same meaning while still receiving task-specific context.

### Keep the free layer self-hosted <!-- (from .agentctx/decisions.md) -->
Status: accepted
Date: YYYY-MM-DD

The free Mind Ontology layer should work locally from Markdown files and a local
MCP server.

Reason:

Developers should be able to inspect and run the context layer they inject into
their AI agents without depending on a hosted service.

## Architecture

### Layer map <!-- (from .agentctx/architecture.md) -->
Describe the layers in your own setup.

Example:

- Source: `.agentctx/` Markdown files.
- Compiler: turns source blocks into task-scoped context packs.
- MCP server: exposes `get_context(task)` and `list_constraints()`.
- AI clients: Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, ChatGPT-compatible clients, or other
  MCP-capable tools.

### Context flow <!-- (from .agentctx/architecture.md) -->
Describe how context should move through the system.

Example:

An AI agent receives a task, calls `get_context(task)`, reads the selected
direction and decisions plus all constraints, then acts inside those boundaries.

### Hosted adapter boundary <!-- (from .agentctx/architecture.md) -->
Describe what is local and what belongs to any hosted service.

Example:

The local ontology is enough for shared direction and constraints. Hosted memory
may add durable retrieval, graph links, and writeback later, but local context
compilation must keep working without it.

## Agent roles

### Strategy agent <!-- (from .agentctx/agent-roles.md) -->
Use this role for product direction, tradeoff analysis, phase planning, and
deciding what should be built next.

### Coding agent <!-- (from .agentctx/agent-roles.md) -->
Use this role for implementation, tests, commits, and PR creation inside the
current task scope.

### Review agent <!-- (from .agentctx/agent-roles.md) -->
Use this role for code review, risk classification, missing test detection, and
merge-readiness assessment.

### Memory agent <!-- (from .agentctx/agent-roles.md) -->
Use this role for turning durable decisions into ontology updates or hosted
memory writeback proposals.

## Glossary

### Context pack <!-- (from .agentctx/glossary.md) -->
A task-scoped bundle of direction, decisions, constraints, and vocabulary that
an AI agent receives before acting.

### Constraint <!-- (from .agentctx/glossary.md) -->
A non-negotiable rule the agent must respect. Constraints are included even
when the task does not appear to match them.

### Decision <!-- (from .agentctx/glossary.md) -->
A durable choice and reason that should guide future work unless it is replaced
by a newer decision.

### Hosted adapter <!-- (from .agentctx/glossary.md) -->
An optional connection from the local ontology to a hosted memory, graph, or
writeback service. The local ontology should still work without it.

## Competency questions

### What should the agent know before starting? <!-- (from .agentctx/cq.md) -->
The ontology should answer which direction, decisions, constraints, and terms
matter for the current task, drawn from `direction.md`, `decisions.md`, and
`glossary.md`.

### What is the current direction this work serves? <!-- (from .agentctx/cq.md) -->
The ontology should state, from `direction.md`, what the operator is building now
so the agent's changes move that direction forward instead of drifting.

### What must the agent avoid? <!-- (from .agentctx/cq.md) -->
The ontology should answer which actions are forbidden, risky, destructive, or
outside the current scope, drawn from `constraints.md`.

### Which writes are forbidden, and when must the agent fail closed? <!-- (from .agentctx/cq.md) -->
The ontology should name the writes the agent must never perform (deploy,
migration, secrets, production config, live data, hosted writeback execution)
and the conditions under which it stops rather than proceeds. See
`constraints.md` and the task risk modes.

### Which files am I allowed to write for this task? <!-- (from .agentctx/cq.md) -->
The ontology should let the agent determine the allowed write surface for the
current task from `direction.md`, `projects.md`, and `constraints.md`, so it
edits only in-scope paths.

### Which prior decision applies? <!-- (from .agentctx/cq.md) -->
The ontology should help the agent find relevant prior decisions in
`decisions.md` without loading every decision into every task.

### Is this capability local or hosted? <!-- (from .agentctx/cq.md) -->
The ontology should clarify whether a capability belongs to the local OSS layer
or an optional, fail-closed hosted adapter — the local path must never be
load-bearing on the hosted one.
